evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design of digital services [...] such as, gambling-like features in video games
stop the practice of the remote disabling of videogames by the publishers, before providing reasonable means to continue functioning of said videogames.

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act at this stage aims to bring about regulation that would rein in no less than 5 different unethical practices. I support more regulation for all of them. I particularly look forward to tighter regulations put on what the DFA refers to as "addictive design of digital services [...] such as, gambling-like features in video games". After spending most of my life engaging with videogames as a hobby and with various communities formed around it; I have come to view the increased use of such features, typically in "free to play" titles, as one of the biggest problems affecting fellow hobbyists. Industry giants profit greatly off of such practices, often at the expense of the consumers' wellbeing. I also support amending the DFA by adding consumer protections in a sixth key area. Namely, I ask for regulations that would stop the practice of the remote disabling of videogames by the publishers, before providing reasonable means to continue functioning of said videogames. Products that I have personally purchased, including Overwatch, Payday 2 and Rocket League (to name but a few) have already either lost all function or had related features and assets become unusable as a result of the publishers' actions. At this very moment, hundreds of videogames are at risk of being effectively destroyed at the publisher's whim. Millions of consumers are at risk of having products they paid for disabled by the publisher. Untold numbers of similar injustices will take place if no legislation is introduced which incentivizes publishers to forgo this practice. At the time of writing, a European Citizen's Initiative titled "Stop Destroying Videogames" has garnered over 1,400,000 signatures because it seeks to have this very practice discussed and hopefully curtailed through regulation by the highest authorities in the European Union. I have long since signed it and introduced it to a number of acquaintances, most of whom have also signed it. When I signed the ECI, it was a first for me. Giving feedback of this kind will also be a first. Many people I know who signed it had not engaged so thoroughly with the minutia of the EU's regulations before or since. It is a shame that that the unethical practice of needlessly leaving videogames in a dysfunctional state has gone unnoticed by the authorities for so long, and by the consumers themselves for almost as long. However, the issue is quickly becoming impossible to ignore. Hundreds of thousands of consumers are calling for the EU to act in their interests, and the Digital Fairness Act presents an opportunity to do just that. I support the DFA as it stands, but I will celebrate the DFA's passing if the European Commission sees fit to make use of this opportunity and amend it in a manner similar to the one I described above.
